developer.name: James Carmichael
developer.title: Psychology Major & Aspiring Criminal Defense Attorney
developer.tagline: Full Ride Scholarship | Future Law School Candidate
developer.bio: A psychology major at Kean University with a passion for understanding the human mind and defending those who need it most. After completing my degree on a full ride scholarship, I plan to pursue law school and become a criminal defense attorney. My background in web development has taught me problem-solving, attention to detail, and the discipline to master complex systems — skills that will serve me well in law.

projects.title: Summer-tainment Site
projects.description: A seasonal entertainment-focused web experience that highlights engaging content, visuals, and interactive elements centered around summer activities and media. Emphasized creative front-end design, user-friendly layouts, and responsive navigation while experimenting with JavaScript-driven interactivity.
projects.reflection: Through this project, skills in page structuring, styling consistency, and visual storytelling were further developed.
projects.process: Started by conceptualizing a summer-themed layout with bright, vibrant colors. Built a modular structure with semantic HTML, then layered custom CSS to create dynamic animations. Added JavaScript to handle interactive elements like image galleries and hover effects.
challenges: Ensuring responsiveness across different screen sizes
Creating smooth transitions without using heavy libraries
outcomes: Successfully created an engaging seasonal experience
Learned importance of planning animations before coding
Proved ability to create visual impact with pure CSS

projects.title: Second Look
projects.description: A redesigned and improved version of a previous web project after encountering technical issues. Focused on problem-solving, debugging, and rebuilding functionality in a more organized and efficient way.
projects.reflection: Demonstrates persistence in software development by taking lessons learned from earlier attempts and implementing cleaner code structure and improved responsiveness.
projects.process: Analyzed the original code to identify structural issues and areas for improvement. Rebuilt from ground up with better HTML semantic structure. Refactored JavaScript to be more modular and maintainable.
challenges: Understanding what went wrong in the original project
Deciding what to keep vs rebuild
Maintaining functionality while improving code quality
outcomes: Successfully rebuilt the project with cleaner code
Learned valuable lessons about planning before building

projects.title: Spooky Ghost
projects.description: A Halloween-themed interactive website using animations, styling effects, and playful visuals to create a fun seasonal experience. Explored JavaScript interactivity while incorporating themed design elements through CSS styling.
projects.reflection: Reflects creativity and technical implementation in front-end development, experimentation with animation timing, hover effects, and engaging visual presentation.
projects.process: Created a spooky visual theme using dark colors, shadows, and ghostly imagery. Built custom CSS animations for floating ghosts, pulsing effects, and eerie transitions. Added JavaScript event listeners for interactive hover states.
challenges: Timing animations to feel organic and spooky
Preventing animation lag on lower-end devices
Creating visual appeal without being too dark/unreadable
outcomes: Demonstrated creative application of CSS animations
Created themed user experience that's visually appealing and technically sound

projects.title: Spooky Spider
projects.description: Continued seasonal web design by creating an interactive Halloween-inspired webpage featuring stylized graphics, animated elements, and responsive visual components. Focused on refining front-end design techniques and strengthening JavaScript functionality.
projects.reflection: Demonstrates growing confidence in creating themed user experiences while maintaining organized and readable code.
projects.process: Built upon lessons from Spooky Ghost with improved structure. Created spider-themed graphics using CSS, implemented complex animation sequences, organized code into logical sections for better maintainability.

challenges: Making animations feel natural while being responsive
outcomes: Learned how to structure JavaScript for maintainability
Kept complex animations smooth and performant

projects.title: World Salad
projects.description: A creatively themed web project centered around combining information, visuals, and interactive elements into a unified experience. Explored layout organization, content presentation, and JavaScript-powered user engagement features.
projects.reflection: Highlights experimentation with both design consistency and dynamic web content while balancing readability and functionality.
projects.process: Conceptualized a 'salad' metaphor for mixing different content types. Designed a flexible grid system to accommodate various content pieces. Built interactive sorting and filtering system using JavaScript.
challenges: Deciding how to organize diverse content types
Maintaining visual coherence while being flexible
Ensuring performance with many DOM elements
outcomes: Successfully organized complex information in an engaging way
Learned value of metaphor in design
Discovered how metaphor guides user understanding

projects.title: Name Generator
projects.description: I built this project because I love the intersection of utility and fun. Name Generator combines powerful randomization with instant gratification—every click delivers a new possibility. What makes this project special is how I engineered it behind the scenes. I organized first names, last names, and middle names into separate arrays, then built intelligent randomization logic that prevents the tedious repetition of consecutive duplicate names. The copy-to-clipboard feature is seamless (because bad UX for a core feature is unforgivable), and the name history tracking lets users revisit their favorite discoveries. I've found this project to be deceptively deep—it taught me that even simple-looking applications require thoughtful architecture. The randomization algorithms, array manipulation, and DOM interactions all had to work together perfectly for the user experience to feel effortless. What I really love about this project is how people actually use it—it's genuinely helpful for writers, game developers, and anyone needing creative naming inspiration.
projects.reflection: This project reinforced my belief that sometimes the best applications are the simplest ones—but only if they're built with careful attention to detail. Name Generator taught me that users don't care how complex your code is; they only care that it works perfectly every single time. The project elevated my confidence in building tools that people would actually want to use, and proved that thoughtful architecture matters even when the feature set is lean.
projects.process: Built arrays of first names, last names, and middle names. Wrote randomization logic to pick from each array. Created UI to display results and regenerate on user click.
challenges: Organizing code with multiple name arrays
outcomes: Proved ability to combine arrays with randomization and DOM manipulation

projects.title: Roshambo
projects.description: Roshambo holds a special place in my portfolio because it represents my first real foray into competitive game programming. I built an AI opponent that actually plays fairly—not a cheap computer that cheats, but one that makes genuinely random choices using algorithms I carefully implemented. The game logic uses conditionals to handle every possible outcome, but what I'm proudest of is the difficulty system. By adjusting the AI's strategy parameters, I created multiple difficulty levels that genuinely challenge players at different skill thresholds. The visual feedback system is key to the experience—when you win, lose, or tie, the application communicates it instantly through animations and color changes. Score tracking is persistent and intuitive, building a sense of progression that keeps players coming back. I invested 12 hours into getting the little details right: animation timing, button responsiveness, edge case handling, and that satisfying feeling when you beat the computer. This project taught me that fun isn't just about features—it's about polish, responsiveness, and making every interaction feel good.
projects.reflection: Roshambo was my classroom for understanding that game programming is about more than logic—it's about creating an engaging loop that keeps players invested. Building a fair AI opponent taught me algorithms and randomization, but crafting the experience around that AI taught me what makes games actually fun. This project proved to me that I could manage complex interactions, state changes, and user feedback simultaneously while maintaining code clarity. Every time I see someone play it and enjoy the experience, I feel validated in the effort I put into those seemingly small details.
projects.process: Designed game logic using if/else conditionals to determine winners. Implemented score tracking system. Created visual feedback for game outcomes. Built AI opponent with variable difficulty.
challenges: Handling edge cases in game logic
outcomes: Proved ability to create engaging games

projects.title: Postcard Generator
projects.description: A creative web application allowing users to generate customized digital postcard-style content through interactive controls and dynamic page updates. Explored DOM manipulation and visual design customization.
projects.reflection: Demonstrates understanding of combining user creativity with functional programming concepts to build engaging browser-based tools.
projects.process: Created template postcard designs. Built form controls to let users customize text, colors, and images. Used JavaScript to dynamically update postcard preview as users make changes.
features: Color picker for backgrounds and text
challenges: Ensuring responsive UI during large file operations
outcomes: Built practical creative tool for users

projects.title: To-Do List
projects.description: This is the project that taught me how to build real applications that people rely on. A to-do list sounds simple, but it's deceptively complex—every feature you add must integrate seamlessly with the core task management system. I engineered local storage persistence so tasks survive browser refreshes (because losing your tasks is a dealbreaker). The filtering system lets users focus on what matters right now, while the sorting by priority and date ensures tasks are accessible in whatever way the user needs. What I really invested time in was the state synchronization—ensuring the UI always reflects the underlying data, no matter what action the user takes. I added keyboard shortcuts for power users, drag-to-reorder for tactile satisfaction, and a dark mode toggle because users deserve choices. The real accomplishment here is that despite all these features, the application still feels clean and intuitive. I learned that managing application state is a skill that extends far beyond to-do lists—it's fundamental to any larger application. This project proved I could build something with enough features to be genuinely useful while maintaining simplicity in the user interface.
projects.reflection: To-Do List was my breakthrough project into building applications that users actually depend on. It taught me that code organization matters exponentially when you're managing persistent state, real user data, and multiple interconnected features. The challenge of keeping the UI synchronized with the data model was intellectually satisfying—it forced me to think deeply about architecture before writing code. Most importantly, this project showed me that good design is about removing friction, not adding features. Every feature I implemented had to justify its existence by genuinely helping users be more productive. This is the project that made me realize I'm building for humans, not just writing code.
projects.process: Designed clean, minimalist UI for task management. Built JavaScript system to handle add, edit, delete, complete operations. Implemented local storage to persist tasks between sessions. Added filtering and sorting features.
challenges: Handling edge cases (empty lists, special characters)
Ensuring performance with hundreds of tasks
outcomes: Demonstrated ability to build applications with persistent data

projects.title: Presidential Website
projects.description: Designed to present informational content in a structured and visually engaging way. Emphasized research presentation, responsive design principles, and organized navigation systems.
projects.reflection: Demonstrates ability to balance educational content with appealing front-end development practices while maintaining accessibility and readability.

projects.title: Monthly Website Gallery
projects.description: Serves as a collection and showcase of web-based projects, highlighting design experimentation and progressive development skills over time. Focused on content organization and gallery layouts.

projects.process: Curated and organized past projects by month. Designed grid-based gallery layout. Built filtering and sorting system. Created project detail pages with descriptions and links.
challenges: Making gallery performant with many images

projects.title: Hangman
projects.description: Hangman was one of my first ventures into game development, and I absolutely love this project. There's something satisfying about taking a timeless game and bringing it to life on the web. I invested significant effort into making the game feel smooth and engaging—from the progressive hangman drawing that updates with each wrong guess, to the visual feedback that makes every letter selection feel rewarding. The word arrays span multiple categories, and I built intelligent state management to handle edge cases like duplicate guesses and win/loss conditions. What I'm most proud of is how polished the experience feels despite being built with vanilla JavaScript. The hint system adds strategic depth, the difficulty levels challenge players at different skill levels, and the game history feature lets players track their progress over time. This project taught me that great game design isn't about complexity—it's about responsiveness, clarity, and that satisfying feedback loop when players interact with your creation.
projects.reflection: Hangman was transformative for my understanding of game development. Beyond just implementing loops and conditionals, I learned how tight game state management directly impacts user experience. The progressive hangman drawing required careful synchronization with the game logic, handling every possible game state without bugs or inconsistencies. This project proved that I could build something entertaining and polished that keeps players engaged—a skill that extends far beyond games into any interactive application. It's a project I still enjoy showing to people because the gameplay speaks for itself.
projects.process: Built word array database. Implemented game logic with letter guessing, wrong guess counting, and win/lose detection. Created hangman drawing progression. Built UI for letter selection.
outcomes: Demonstrated understanding of loops and conditionals

projects.title: State Facts Objects Practice
projects.description: Focused on practicing object-oriented data organization and information display using facts about U.S. states. Explored JavaScript objects, arrays, and structured data handling.
projects.reflection: Demonstrates growing confidence in managing and displaying structured information dynamically within a webpage.
projects.process: Created object-based data structure for each state with properties like capital, population, area, facts. Built UI to display state information. Implemented search and filter functionality.
challenges: Organizing complex data with multiple properties
Dynamically updating UI based on data
Making object structures easy to maintain
outcomes: Built practical system for managing structured information
Proved ability to organize complex data logically

projects.title: Star Wars Part 2

Creating smooth transitions between story beats

projects.title: Wildcard Website
projects.description: An experimental platform for testing new ideas, layouts, and interactive concepts in web development. Combined multiple front-end techniques including custom styling and JavaScript functionality.
projects.reflection: Demonstrates adaptability and willingness to explore unconventional approaches to design and functionality.
projects.process: Created experimental testing ground for new techniques and ideas. Tried unconventional layout approaches. Tested new animation and interaction patterns. Documented findings.
outcomes: Identified techniques that became standard in later projects

projects.title: FlicksByNick
projects.description: FlicksByNick represents a significant leap forward in my design capabilities. I spent 22 hours building a professional-grade movie platform because I wanted to prove to myself that I could create something that looks like it was designed by professionals. The CSS work here is meticulous—every button, card, and transition was carefully crafted for visual cohesion and smoothness. The responsive media grid adapts beautifully from desktop to mobile, and the movie filtering and search system is intelligently engineered for performance even with hundreds of movies loaded. What I love about this project is that it's not just technically sound—it's genuinely beautiful. I obsessed over color schemes, typography, spacing, and animation timings because I understood that these details are what separate amateur projects from professional ones. The trailer integration pulls external media seamlessly, the ratings display gives users social proof, and the recommendation engine uses simple but effective algorithms to suggest movies users might enjoy. I put genuine care into the user experience at every level, from the initial visual impression to the smallest interaction feedback. This project is evidence that I can design and build at a professional level.
projects.reflection: FlicksByNick was my inflection point into professional-level design. I realized that technical competence is just the baseline—what separates good projects from great ones is the attention to every detail, the willingness to iterate on visual polish, and the obsessive care for the user experience. This project taught me that design is a discipline worth taking seriously, that performance matters when handling media at scale, and that professional applications require thinking about edge cases, load times, and user accessibility. I'm proud of this work because it represents the moment I stopped building 'projects' and started building 'products'.
projects.process: Designed professional movie platform interface. Built advanced CSS for polished appearance. Implemented complex media display system. Created intuitive content discovery system.
challenges: Ensuring fast load times with many images
outcomes: Significantly improved design and UX skills
Demonstrated ability to integrate external media effectively

projects.title: Movies
projects.description: Movies was my opportunity to build a cinematic experience that matches the gravity of film itself. I invested 18 hours into creating an interface that feels like stepping into a theater, not a spreadsheet. The visual theme is deliberate—dark backgrounds with vibrant accent colors that make each movie pop, typography that commands attention, and layouts that breathe with intentional whitespace. The grid responds beautifully to any screen size, never breaking, never feeling cramped. The search and filtering system is fast and intuitive—users find exactly what they want without friction. I'm particularly proud of the sorting capabilities (by rating, year, popularity) because they give power users the tools to curate their experience. The watchlist feature uses intelligent state management to persist selections, the ratings system builds community signals, and the smooth transitions between states make every interaction feel satisfying. This project taught me that building for specific domains (in this case, movie enthusiasts) means understanding their needs deeply and anticipating their mental models. I didn't just build a database display—I built an experience that respects the medium of film and the passion of people who love movies.
projects.reflection: Movies proved that I could maintain design consistency and technical rigor across a complex, media-rich application. The challenge of keeping performance smooth while rendering multiple full-resolution images taught me about optimization and lazy loading. More importantly, this project showed me that I could build applications that feel tailored to their purpose—in this case, a platform for people passionate about cinema. The intersection of technical execution, thoughtful design, and deep understanding of user needs is where I found the most satisfaction in this project. This is the kind of work I'm genuinely proud to show people.
projects.process: Built cinematic-themed interface for movie browsing. Designed responsive layout for all screen sizes. Implemented interactive features like movie filtering and sorting.
challenges: Maintaining design consistency across responsive breakpoints
Implementing smooth interactions without performance degradation
outcomes: Proved ability to handle complex interactive requirements

projects.title: NJIT Project

reflection.overall: Three years in the Web Design Pathway Program taught me discipline, creativity, and problem-solving—skills that now shape my journey in psychology and criminal law. Web design showed me how important it is to understand people's needs, and criminal defense requires the same empathy, critical thinking, and dedication to protecting the rights and experiences of future clients.
reflection.challenges: The hardest moments came from imposter syndrome and learning how to work through difficult problems without giving up. Over time, I realized that challenges are opportunities to grow and gain understanding. That mindset will help me in criminal law, where analyzing behavior, interpreting legal precedent, and navigating the justice system all require patience and strong problem-solving skills.
reflection.favorites: My favorite memory was realizing I could create something meaningful that changes how people experience and interact with the world. That feeling inspired me to pursue a deeper purpose: defending constitutional rights and helping people navigate the criminal justice system with compassion, understanding, and the determination to make a difference.
reflection.transformation: Web design and psychology have given me a unique perspective by teaching me to understand both systems and human behavior. Through these experiences, I've grown into someone who wants to create meaningful change. I'm no longer only building interfaces—I'm preparing to advocate for justice and support people through difficult moments in their lives.

futurePlans.college: Majoring in Psychology at Kean University on a full ride scholarship for 4 years.
futurePlans.career: Planning to attend law school to earn my Juris Doctor degree and become a criminal defense attorney.
goals: Complete my psychology degree with honors
Prepare for and excel on the LSAT
Build a career defending clients' rights
futurePlans.message: It's never to prove anybody wrong... I like proving my support system right, the people who really believe in me, I like proving them right. No matter what it is, I got traded, I always try to keep a positive outlook.
